name: s3fs (S3-backed Filesystem Interface for Python)
pricing_notes: Open source (BSD-3); fsspec ecosystem; AWS S3 API costs apply

auth.notes: AWS credentials via boto3 chain (env vars, ~/.aws/credentials, IAM role)

performance.notes: S3 API calls per operation; caching available; parallel transfers via open_file()

agent_readiness.notes: Filesystem-like interface to S3. import s3fs. fs = s3fs.S3FileSystem(). with fs.open('my-bucket/file.parquet', 'rb') as f: df = pd.read_parquet(f). fs.ls('my-bucket/'). fs.glob('my-bucket/**/*.csv'). fs.rm('my-bucket/old-file.csv'). fs.cp('my-bucket/src', 'my-bucket/dst'). Pandas direct: pd.read_parquet('s3://my-bucket/file.parquet'). Dask/Zarr: native integration. Async: s3fs.S3FileSystem(asynchronous=True). Requester pays. MinIO compatible. Part of fsspec — provides uniform interface across S3, GCS, Azure, local.

gotchas: Each ls() call makes S3 API requests — can be expensive for deep directory trees
No atomic transactions — write operations are not transactional
Cache invalidation required when S3 objects change externally
For high-performance parallel reads, prefer boto3 directly with concurrent.futures
best_when: Reading/writing Parquet, CSV, Zarr directly to S3 with pandas/dask/xarray
When code should work with both local and S3 paths transparently
avoid_when: Large file uploads with multipart (boto3 S3Transfer more efficient)
Operations requiring S3 presigned URLs or ACL management
summary: s3fs provides a filesystem-like interface to AWS S3. fs = s3fs.S3FileSystem(). fs.open('bucket/file.parquet', 'rb') for reading. fs.ls(), fs.glob(), fs.rm(), fs.cp(). pandas/dask/xarray use s3fs transparently via pd.read_parquet('s3://bucket/file'). Part of fsspec — uniform interface across S3, GCS, Azure, local. Async support. MinIO compatible. For high-performance parallel transfers use boto3 TransferManager directly.
